title: Integration, Not Memorization
content: The bottleneck in personal growth isn't **acquiring** new ideas — it's **integrating** the ones you already have. Most people consume endlessly and apply almost nothing. The gap between knowing something and living it is where all the value is. Spaced repetition closes that gap. Each review isn't asking "do you remember this?" — it's asking **"are you living this yet?"** Use the buttons to tune how often you encounter each idea: - 🔴 **Again** → I want this every day until I'm embodying it. - 🟠 **Hard** → Bring this back sooner — I need more time with it. - 🟢 **Good** → The timing feels right for where I am with this idea. - 🔵 **Easy** → This is deeply absorbed. I can go longer without seeing it. You're not being tested. You're tuning the frequency. Some ideas need heavy rotation for weeks. Others click after one read. Trust your gut — you know what needs more time.

title: You Become What You Repeatedly Encounter
content: Your information diet shapes your worldview. But it's not just what you consume — it's what you **revisit**. An idea encountered once is noise. An idea encountered five times across three months becomes a lens you see through. MindFeed is a deliberate information diet for your deepest thinking. Add: - Insights you want to embody more fully in your life - Ideas you want to articulate clearly and confidently - Frameworks you want to apply naturally in your decision-making - Quotes that compress hard-won wisdom into a line - Passages from books, conversations, or podcasts that shifted something in you Just tell your AI: "add this to my MindFeed." The goal: a curated garden of the ideas, wisdom, and frameworks you want shaping how you see the world — resurfaced at the right time.

title: The Spacing Effect
content: Spaced repetition exploits the **spacing effect** — we retain and integrate information better when encounters are spread across time rather than clustered together. The forgetting curve (Ebbinghaus, 1885) shows memory decays exponentially, but each review resets and flattens the curve. Optimal intervals grow geometrically: 1 day → 3 days → 7 days → 21 days → 60 days. This isn't just about memory. Each time you revisit an idea, you bring new experiences since the last review. The idea connects differently. You notice implications you missed. Integration happens in the gaps between reviews, not during them. 2-5 minutes daily. That's 30 cards reviewed in a week, each one a small recalibration of how you think. Over months, the ideas you keep in rotation become the default lenses you see through — not because you memorized them, but because you gave them enough time to become part of you.
